822	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
823	
824	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
825	reduces, via the locality criterion
826	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
827	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
828	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
829	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
830	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
831	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
832	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
833	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
834	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
835	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
836	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
837	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
838	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
839	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
840	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
841	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	
845	\leanok
846	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
847	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
849	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
850	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
851	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
852	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
853	  % L920--926).
854	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
855	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
856	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
857	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
858	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
859	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
860	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
861	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
862	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
863	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
864	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
865	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
866	  by the affine-open locality criterion
867	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
868	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
869	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
870	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
871	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
872	
873	\end{lemma}
874	
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
877	  By the affine-open locality criterion
878	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
879	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
880	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
881	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
882	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
883	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
884	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
885	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
886	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
887	
888	  \medskip
889	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
890	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
891	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
892	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
893	  \[
894	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
895	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
896	  \]
897	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
898	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
899	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
900	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
901	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
902	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
903	
904	  \medskip
905	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
906	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
907	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
908	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
909	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
910	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
911	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
912	  \begin{itemize}
913	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
914	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
915	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
916	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
917	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
918	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
919	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
920	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
921	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
922	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
923	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
924	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
925	  \end{itemize}
926	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
927	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
928	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
929	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
930	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
931	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
932	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
933	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
934	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
935	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
936	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
937	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
938	  obligation beyond them.
939	
940	  \medskip
941	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
942	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
943	  that the base-change map restricts to an isomorphism on sections over each affine
944	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2, precisely the
945	  affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with \emph{no} flatness
946	  hypothesis, by the section-level computation
947	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
948	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
949	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme

64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in

100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}

346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,

353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}

355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.

368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean; the present theorem is a closed, axiom-clean Lean declaration
408	  %   (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational). The ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}

430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,

432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}

4005	  \textit{The affine equivalence QCoh\((\Spec R)\) \(\simeq\) Mod\((R)\): a quasi-coherent module
4006	  sheaf on \(\Spec R\) is \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M)}\) up to canonical isomorphism.}
4007	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) that is quasi-coherent
4008	  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism;

4018	  Since \(M\) is quasi-coherent, the section-localization descent

4020	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over

4029	\texorpdfstring{$\widetilde{(-)}$}{tilde}-counit}

4032	\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\): given that the section restriction localizes on every basic
4033	open, the tilde-Gamma counit is an isomorphism. Together with its converse

4035	\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}, which is exactly what makes G1-core

4037	(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) interderivable -- the route by which G1-core is obtained as

4068	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}

4073	  \(S\) are canonically isomorphic by a linear equivalence \(M' \cong M''\) compatible with \(g\)
4074	  and \(g'\).

4078	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}

4090	  \label{lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations}

4092	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl is `private` in the source (a project-internal helper for the

4094	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}
4095	  \textit{Linear-algebra glue for the G1-assemble step.}
4096	  Let \(S \subseteq R\) be a submonoid and let \(g : M \to M'\), \(g' : M \to M''\) both exhibit a
4097	  localization of \(M\) at \(S\). If a linear map \(h : M' \to M''\) satisfies \(h \circ g = g'\),

4099	\end{lemma}

4105	  coincide by uniqueness of maps out of a localization
4106	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}). A linear equivalence is bijective, hence so
4107	  is \(h\).

2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}

2461	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2462	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}

2466	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2467	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2468	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2469	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2470	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2471	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2472	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2473	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2474	\end{lemma}

2476	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2477	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}

2490	  % gap1 into all three IsLocalizedModule fields at once. gap1 itself reduces to the
2491	  % section-localization descent (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}, keystone D), built from the
2492	  % slice-to-geometric bridge (\cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}, C) and the per-element local-tilde

2494	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2495	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2496	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2497	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2498	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2499	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2500	  %  associated to $M$.
2501	  %  [...]
2502	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2503	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2504	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2505	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2506	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2507	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2508	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2509	  \begin{enumerate}
2510	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2511	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2512	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2513	    \item the section-restriction map
2514	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2515	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2516	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2517	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2518	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2519	  \end{enumerate}
2520	\end{lemma}
2521	
2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2527	
2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2546	\end{proof}

2549	[Basic-open restriction of a $\widetilde{N}$ sheaf is a module localization]

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	
48	\leanok
49	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
50	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}

3790	  \(e_1 : M_1 \simeq_+ N_1\) and \(e_2 : M_2 \simeq_+ N_2\) are additive isomorphisms that are
3791	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear (\(e_i(a \cdot x) = \sigma(a) \cdot e_i(x)\)), and \(h : N_1 \to N_2\) is an
3792	  \(R'\)-linear map intertwining them, \(h(e_1 x) = e_2(g x)\). Then \(h\) satisfies
3793	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\ (S.\mathrm{map}\ \sigma)\ h\) over \(R'\).

3799	  (\(\mathrm{Module.End.isUnit\_iff}\)). \emph{surj:} given \(y \in N_2\), pull back \(e_2^{-1}y\), apply
3800	  the surjectivity field of \(g\) to get \(g\,m = s\cdot e_2^{-1}y\), and push forward by \(e_2\); the
3801	  image-submonoid witness is \(\langle \sigma s, s, s\in S, \mathrm{rfl}\rangle\). \emph{exists\_of\_eq:}
3802	  symmetric, pulling the equality back through \(e_1\). Mathlib provides only the same-ring

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}

51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
